Crigler-Najjar syndrome, type 2 is a rare inherited disorder that affects the way bilirubin is processed in the body. Bilirubin is a yellow substance that is produced when red blood cells are broken down. Normally, the liver changes bilirubin into a form that can be passed from the body in stool. In people with Crigler-Najjar syndrome, type 2, the liver cannot change bilirubin into a form that can be passed from the body. As a result, bilirubin builds up in the body and causes jaundice, which is a yellowing of the skin and whites of the eyes.

Systemic scleroderma is a group of autoimmune diseases that cause the skin and sometimes internal organs to harden and tighten. The two main types are localized scleroderma, which affects only the skin, and systemic scleroderma, which can affect the skin, blood vessels, muscles, and internal organs. The exact cause of systemic scleroderma is unknown, but it is thought to involve a combination of genetic and environmental factors. Atrial fibrillation (AF) is a type of arrhythmia, or abnormal heart rhythm. In AF, the heart's upper chambers, the atria, beat irregularly and out of sync with the heart's lower chambers, the ventricles. This can cause blood to pool and clot in the heart. If a blood clot forms, it can travel to the brain and cause a stroke. AF is the most common type of serious arrhythmia. Supravalvular aortic stenosis is caused by mutations in the NOTCH1 gene. This gene provides instructions for making a protein that is involved in many cell signaling pathways. The NOTCH1 protein is found in many tissues and organs, including the heart. In the heart, the NOTCH1 protein is involved in the development of the aortic valve, which is one of four valves that controls blood flow through the heart. The NOTCH1 protein is also involved in the development of the aortic arch, which is the blood vessel that carries oxygen-rich blood from the heart to the rest of the body.
